results.possibleIdentity: James F. Umland (1928–2016) was an American conceptual artist, teacher, and radio host who was a significant figure in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in Appleton, Wisconsin, he earned a BFA from the University of Iowa and a Master's degree from New York University. Umland was closely associated with the emerging conceptual art movement and the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, participating in their exhibitions between 1968 and 1969. His work is documented in Lucy Lippard's seminal book, 'Six Years: The Dematerialization of the Art Object from 1966 to 1972.' He was a friend and collaborator of artist Robert Barry and worked with other luminaries such as John Cage and Dary John Mizelle. Beyond his visual art, Umland hosted a radio program on WBAI-FM in New York focused on the art of creative invention, where he interviewed prominent figures like Dave Brubeck and Jacques D'Amboise. His professional career also included teaching art in high schools and colleges in the New York City area until his retirement. His work has been exhibited at major institutions, including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Brooklyn Museum, and the Westerly Gallery.
